comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Edward Gorey -- yes, that Edward Gorey, the author of The Gashlycrumb Tinies and scores of other quirkily illustrated books. Among his omnivorous interests as balletomane, cinephile, maniacal collector of books, cat fancier, and more, he was a member of the American Society for Psychical Research and a serious student of divination, with a particular interest in the I Ching. While a novelty divination deck (the Fantod Deck) was made of his illustrations after his death, I consider it a real pity that he never crafted his own Tarot deck; it would have been an astonishing thing. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- yes, this means you! See you next week.***

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
4) I've done some experimenting with lucid dreaming.

Before I started, I hadn't recalled a single dream in 10-20 years. My main means of breaking my dream drought was a mixture of affirmations and a dream journal. I got these techniques from Complete Book of Lucid Dreaming by Clare R Johnson.

First, I had to make up my mind to make room in my sleeping time for dream-related practices. The bed is warm and comfy at 2am, so you have to accept that you will be groaning, rolling over, and making half-awake scribbles at odd parts of the night and there isn't anything for it but to grit your teeth and do it. It is easier to do this when you've consciously decided you're on board.

Then I put a blank notebook, pen, and pen flashlight within reach of my sleeping position. The flashlight was easier than fumbling with the light switch, and a penlight clips easily to the notebook with your pen for added storage convenience.

Next, whenever I got into bed for the night, I would repeat the affirmation "I remember my dreams" in my head three times and relax as much as possible. Any time a stray train of thought would start up, I would notice it and let it go so I would go to sleep as quickly as possible.

Then whenever I woke up, I would immediately grab my journal from its place and think back to the moment before. I wrote down any stray thoughts that come to mind, just doing the best I could. My dreams came in bits and pieces at first, but as I went on it got easier, so just keep at it.

Between those two practices, I was able to go from no dreams at all to one or two every night a few weeks later. After I let my journaling go, it went down to one or two a week.

Lastly, I notice you had some concerns about finishing what you started. Due to the need to interrupt precious sleep time for several weeks, I would finish whatever you wanted to finish before doing the above, especially LRM which IIRC had a before bed journaling component that could fight for time with your dream journal.

Currently psychedelics are very popular in the occult scene. This makes me wonder the esoteric dimension of psychedelics -- it seems that some people have mystic experiences on psychedelics. Others have bad trips. Yet others simply party.

1) What is your sense of how psychedelics function esoterically.

2) how do psychedelics effect the subtle bodies?

3) What are the effects of a bad trip on the surrounding space? I wonder if rampant psychedelic abuse contributes to how nasty the energy at college campuses are...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) They temporarily disarrange the relationship between the various subtle bodies so that the ordinary consciousness gets a glimpse of some part of the astral plane.

2) Dion Fortune has a great quote here, from Sane Occultism:

"It is one thing to 'unloose the girders of the mind' and quite another to get the rivets tightened up again, and unless one is prepared to go through life rattling like a cheap motor-car it is unwise to seek this method of development."

I've met quite a few people who used drugs to try to get where meditation and spiritual practice gets you, but without the effort; it's fair to say that all of them rattled like cheap motor-cars, and quite a few had parts falling off...

3) Any strong emotional experience charges the psychic atmosphere of a place, and a bad trip -- which basically amounts to a temporary psychotic break -- can do that very effectively. Yes, that could be part of what makes college campuses so energetically poisonous these days.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 3) As a former college professor, I think there's a bigger factor: no one wants to be there. Not the students, not the professors, and certainly not the staff. Take that many people who desperately want to be somewhere else, cram them together, and make sure to hold it together with toxic debts, and it's no wonder the place goes septic....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Former college librarian here: yup. The students are there for a job certification (and a little partying on the side), the faculty are there to do their own research, the rest of the world be damned (and maybe a little teaching on the side, but only as much as is absolutely required), and the staff are often there because especially in college towns, that may be most of their chance at the employment market. No one is there for the reasons for which higher education ostensibly exists.

As a bit of sauce on top, humanities programs across the country now develop enormous swollen senses of grievance or guilt in their students, while technical or scientific schools inculcate horrible material arrogance and greed.

replies.title: psychedelics
replies.content:
One might contrast the life paths of Richard Albert, who became Ram Dass, and his partner at Harvard, Timothy Leary. Albert took the insights of LSD and turned to a heavily spiritual path under the guidance of an experienced guru. Leary did not. I wouldn't say that Leary's career was completely misguided, but he did manage to stumble into far more serious trouble, including prison in several nations.

OTH I always get a laugh out of the way he managed his escape from the California system. All incoming inmates are given physical and psychological exams at the medical facility. Based on those exams and the seriousness of their crime, they are assigned to prisons with different levels of security. Leary's personality profile showed that he would be a cooperative inmate so he was assigned to a low security prison, from which he escaped a short time later. At this point someone noticed that he was the co-author of the personality test. Duh, he knew exactly how to answer to give any impression he wanted to. Blind bureaucracy is always amusing. However, he was recaptured and assigned to the much more secure Folsom Prison.

2) Daily hoodoo cleansing baths (as described in the Magic Monday FAQ) can have some of the same effect, but no, a daily cold shower won't do it, though it'll do some good; to do misogi shuho, the Shinto water purification, you need a river or the sea. A waterfall is even better!

replies.title: (no subject)

1) You're getting stuck on purely material forms of the elements. That's a common beginner's mistake. The elements aren't just the physical substances that represent them and give them their common names. Furthermore, if you're at the South Pole, and your blood and tissues haven't yet frozen solid, you know what? There's liquid water present.

2) I've never known any two Druids who follow exactly the same set of practices. That said, the measure of your commitment to any spiritual path can be gauged quite well by the amount of time you put into that path on a daily basis. If you do five minutes of work a day, you'll get five minutes a day worth of benefit from it.

The astral plane is only one of a series of planes -- seven, in the most common taxonomy -- "above" (that is, subtler than) the physical plane. comments.title: Christian Druidry
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A slight bit of bio before my question. My mother is Catholic (very much a practicing one) and my father is not, but was a Rosicrucian. He agreed to not get in the way of my religious upbringing and so I was raised Catholic and he never discussed any Rosicrucian ideas with me (they were/are happily married, so these different realms always co-existed for me). I actually had no idea what they were about at all, but his personal library always seemed mysterious, interesting, and part of my heritage (his father and a couple of my great aunts were also Rosicrucians). I continued to just see this as somewhat forbidden material, even when my father lost interest in the order a decade or so ago and decided to get rid of his books (he is a minimalist and I am not, so I took them as they seemed important, even if I wouldn't ever read them).

For my part, I lost my faith in Catholicism as a teenager after I stopped attending church and became disillusioned with some of the teachings (primarily with the concept of original sin). I became agnostic, at first leaning towards atheism but never quite there, but I was seeking some answers and eventually pieced together my own sense of what seemed right. It was just an assortment of beliefs, and in the background I never stopped puzzling over the teachings of Christ and the ideas in the bible, eventually coming to my own understanding of the words that allowed me to increasingly make use of them. Many of my beliefs seemed incompatible with Christianity as I understood it, but through your writings and Dion Fortune's work I have come to realize that that they may be quite compatible with each other.

It was quite strange for me when you mentioned that Fortune incorporated aspects of Rosicrucian teachings in the Cosmic Doctrine since I have been finding that book incredibly helpful with my thinking (even though my understanding is still shallow I am sure). It made me wonder what was lurking in those old books I had.

I read with great interest, also, a comment from you in which you posted a diagram from Mansions Of The Soul and I managed to find a copy of that book. When it arrived I had a very strange aversion to it, quite unusual for me, short lived as it turned out, but very strong. A bad energy or something that made me sure I would never open it, but then after putting it out of sight for a few hours I picked it up and read it in a couple of days. This was a great moment for me (so thank you very much for your part in it!!) as incredibly I discovered many of my ideas about life that I'd hand assembled, as it were, from the ideas others but also my own inner contemplation and understandings, just lined up so perfectly with Rosicrucian teachings. A truly strange experience that I felt I had to convey to you somehow.

The element to my thinking that I would like to understand better is my interest in the earth in relation to this, and I must confess I am more convinced in your description of reincarnation leading towards another higher life form rather than directly to heaven. The chasm between man and God seems too large to me still and I am certain there is so much more to the story.

I wondered if you might recommend any books that discuss Christian Druidry, or if you might have any general advice based on what I've told you. I feel hopeful now that there might be a particular cosmos that I actually am aligned with, so to speak, something that runs through and can unify all of my beliefs. I should say I intend to read my dad's collection of books and speak to him about this (not in that order) - I imagine he has been rather amused watching me over the past three decades or so. It might be as strange a moment for him though as it was for my mom when I asked her if she had an extra Rosary recently.

Thanks,
Johnny

replies.title: Re: Christian Druidry
replies.content: Well, if you find Rosicrucian teachings to be unusually meaningful, might I suggest that you consider Rosicrucianism as an option? There are upwards of a dozen active Rosicrucian orders in the western world today; for what it's worth, I'm a member of two of them, one admitting only Master Masons, the other open to people of good character more generally, and have learned a lot from those affiliation. You may find that the path you want is right in front of you...

replies.title: Re: Christian Druidry
replies.content:
Who are the Rosicrucians? I have heard them mentioned a lot but I don’t know anything about them?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Christian Druidry
replies.content: Okay, the Rosicrucian movement got started in Germany and Britain in the late 16th and early 17th century among Protestant Christians -- Lutheran and Anglican, mostly -- who were influenced by the esoteric Christian mysticism of Jacob Boehme and wanted to build a bridge between their faith and the magical traditions of the Renaissance. The word "Rosicrucian" comes from Latin for "rose cross," the cross with a rose at its center (or a circle of roses surrounding the center) being their ancient emblem. There have been many different Rosicrucian orders over the years, claiming descent in various ways from the original Rosicrucians; some of them may have such a lineage, others were pretty clearly invented out of whole cloth, but all of them share a broad common tradition. As I noted to OP, there are upwards of a dozen Rosicrucian orders still around today, some still closely affiliated with Protestant Christianity, others distinct from that current.

replies.title: Re: Christian Druidry
replies.content: Thank you very much JMG. They sound like something very interesting.

replies.title: Re: Christian Druidry
replies.content: JMG, if I may ask: to which do you belong? Which would you recommend for the curious novice? Conversely, of which of them would steer clear?

replies.title: Re: Christian Druidry
replies.content: I'm an initiate of the Societas Rosicruciana in Civitatibus Foederatis (Rosicrucian Society in the United States, SRICF), which is an invitation-only body for Freemasons, but also the Societas Rosicruciana in America (Rosicrucian Society in America, SRIA), which is open to anyone who's interested; I'd recommend both of those, and you can find quite a few books by the founder of the latter. George Winslow Plummer, online -- worth reading to get an idea of the order's flavor. The Rosicrucian Fellowship founded by Max Heindel is also a very solid organization if you don't mind vegetarianism and celibacy -- Heindel borrowed a lot from the Theosophists. I have no personal experience of the Fraternitas Rosae Crucis (Fraternity of the Rosy Cross, FRC) which was headed for many years by the lively R. Swinburne Clymer, but what I've heard about it has been highly favorable.  The Ancient Mystical Order Rosae Crucis (AMORC), the biggest of the Rosicrucian orders, is a very mixed bag and I'm not at all sure I'd recommend them.

Last Friday I didn't get to my ritual practice until after 1:30 pm and I had already smoked a bowl with our new arrival. In a nutshell, it was absolutely brilliant. Miles ahead of any other experience. The flow of the ritual was natural and perfectly paced. I could hold all trains of thought comfortably and simultaneously. I seemed to have an innate comprehension of the whys and wherefores connected to different aspects of the ritual that I haven't studied yet. I even got my vibration into the Yesod sphere for the first time, not to mention hitting all the others squarely. Frankly I was blown away.

BUT, I also think it was a test. I was shown a way I could cheat and achieve a level of vision and understanding I hadn't earned. I can't help but think that there was a (probably rather unsavory) entity behind the rapid advancement, someone who wants to keep me high, and not growing spiritually. I think that I can get to that level on my own, without the inhaled assistance, but it will require discipline, will, and determination to do so. And time.

In a way I think the entity made a mistake with me. I saw what was possible, and it was beautiful. And now I want it. But I want to attain it the right way, in a way that actually makes it mine, and not someone else's lure. (That rarely ends well for the fish!) The best journal dialogue I've ever had with my subconscious followed yesterday, after I'd had a couple days to mull it over. I was able to understand just how subservient I was to the smoke - what it was keeping me FROM, not what it could do FOR me. The veil slipped a little, and I caught a glimpse of a siren we might as well call Mary Jane, revealed for what she was, and even learned a few bars of her song.

comments.title: UFOs and Occultists
comments.content:
JMG – in some of your entries and responses in this blog, you have mentioned that occultists were deliberately active in the creation or cultivation of the UFO phenomenon of the latter half of the 20th century.
Two questions have come to my mind based on this:
1 - Why did they do it (i.e., what was their motive)?
And
2 – Based on your understanding of the phenomenon, were these occultists successful in achieving what they set out to do (keeping in mind the diversity of its manifestations in American pop culture including the ridiculous but incredibly persistent Area 51 myth)?

Many thanks for tirelessly replying to all the MM questions that come your way every week!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: UFOs and Occultists
replies.content:
1) There were two phases to the project, and each had its own motive. The first phase, in 1946 and 1947, was to make nuclear war less likely by convincing as many people as possible that another world was watching. Think of that great bit in Sartre where he has you imagine yourself spying through a keyhole, watching something on the other side; then you hear a noise behind you that makes you aware that somebody has been watching you the whole time. All of a sudden you become aware of how you look in the eyes of the Other, and your behavior and attitude shift suddenly. That's the shift in awareness they wanted to encourage. Later, from 1952 into the 1960s, the goal was to use interest in UFOs to spread occult teachings into the general populace.

2) We'll never know how much the first project had to do with the fact that the US and the Soviet Union didn't go to war early on, when neither side had enough nukes to deter the other from attacking. It may have helped. The second project was a thumping success, and played a large role in launching an impressive revival of occultism across the western world.

replies.title: Re: UFOs and Occultists
replies.content:
Utterly fascinating! Thanks so much!!!


Ron M

comments.title: The Art and Practice of Geomancy
comments.content:
JMG, some weeks ago I discovered your book, The Art and Practice of Geomancy. It resonated greatly with me and has aided in getting past a spiritual plateau that I was on for the last couple years, so many thanks for it and for popularizing this lovely technique.

I had a few questions on the magical side of the book, if you will permit me:

1. You note that a geomantic figure's hour is strengthened on the day ruled by the figure's planet. (For example, Fortuna Major is associated with the sun, and so its hour is especially potent on a Sunday.) I did not see any discussion of Caput Draconis or Cauda Draconis, however! Are these strengthened on Mondays, or the day closest to the transit of the moon over its respective node, or something else?

2. Similarly, if conducting a ritual related to one of those two figures, should I simply use lunar materials and symbolism (e.g. silver/quartz/blue/etc.), or do they have special associations?

3. I have long practiced origami (decorative paper folding) for pleasure. Would such craft be appropriate for the creation of (presumably short duration) talismans? If so, do you know of any lore or rules of thumb that should be applied in doing so? (e.g. is there any issue with sigils crossing creases, or being internal to the final figure (and therefore not visible), etc.?)

Thanks again.

replies.title: Re: The Art and Practice of Geomancy
replies.content:
1) If you look into the section on geomantic hours, you'll find that those two figures are assigned certain hours each week.

2) They correspond astrologically to the Moon's North (Caput) and South (Cauda) nodes, which don't really have a developed symbolism in Western astrological magic. You could try lunar symbolism, certainly.

3) I've never heard of anyone doing this, so you're basically on your own. See what results you get!

replies.title: Re: The Art and Practice of Geomancy
replies.content: Thank you for the responses—sounds like I have some interesting work ahead of me!

comments.title: The nature of time
comments.content:
Happy Monday! Thank you for giving us a reason to look forward to Mondays.

This is more of a metaphysical query than a strictly magical one; I hope that's alright. My question has to do with the nature of time and its relationship to free will (to the extent that we are able to exert it).

It seems that there are two broad conceptions of time:
1) It is real, meaning that the future does not exist, and each instant is created by the actions of the present on the past, which is itself the summation of innumerable present moments. In this case, the scope for willed action is clear. In fact if one considers all matter to have some degree of consciousness, then you could even say that Will creates time by creating a future different than the present. It certainly appears that the actions we chose in the past had an effect on the (then) future, so we should expect that our present actions will have a comparable effect.

2) It is an illusion. Past, present, and future are all equally real, but for some reason, we can only remember the past, perceive the present, and imagine the future, all with varying degrees of fidelity. Metaphysically, there seem to be a number of instances where we get feedback from the future, which shouldn't be possible if it doesn't exist. There is divination of course, but also the concept of destiny, and you've mentioned several times about egregores in the present being affected by what people will do with them in the future. One could argue that divination is looking at probabilities rather than an actual future, but the other two situations seem to imply a real extant future.

Given that both points of view have strong arguments, perhaps a solution exists with elements of both.

3) The universe, past, present, and future, exists with time as one of its dimensions. However, when it came into being, its state was determined by what I imagine as a simultaneous solution of equations with simple entities like elementary particles represented by relatively simple functions, with increasingly complex entities such as a rock, a paramecium, a human, a god, having progressively more complex functions, with Will being part of this function. So, we have a degree of will, and used it as part of the formation of the universe, with my Will, your Will, the lesser Will of the microbes, and the greater Will of the gods all interacting to produce the one unique solution describing the universe that is, essentially writing our lines and directions that we are acting out now.

4) Or, a little closer to case (1), both past and future are real, but the past is fixed, and the future is not. Taking some inspiration from quantum physics, where on a small enough scale, what seems to be solid matter acts like a cloud of probabilities, and even the unlikely probabilities have a real effect, the future is similarly probabilistic, with the more probable futures having a stronger effect on the present. So, even if a highly probable future is pulling in one direction (destiny), assuming the past (fate) gives you the option, it can be avoided with sufficient Will.

Is there a consensus on this topic in occult philosophy? Is this something you have an opinion on? I know The Cosmic Doctrine definitely plays in this territory, though it seems to me to be closer to (1) than (2) with its emphasis on evolution and change. (I am reading along with the group, but I have not finished it.) It does seem that my functions could be another way of describing what the CD describes as movements through space, e.g. of the various atoms. I know that at least some physicists are in the (2) camp; I listened to a physics talk on the nature of time a couple of years ago, and it seemed that the presenter was claiming that because our particle physics models are time-symmetric, then the universe is too, which seems to me like a fine example of the map-is-not-the-territory fallacy. Amusingly, the physicists are in bed with the Calvinists in their beliefs about time, though via completely different reasoning.

replies.title: Re: The nature of time
replies.content:
I tend to see things in a different way.

Time is real, but there are beings who are outside of time. (That's what "eternal" means -- not lasting forever, but entirely outside of time.) When an eternal being looks at time from the outside, it can see everything that happens along the entire course of time, not because it's predetermined, but because -- being outside of time -- such a being can see you make each choice as you make it, and can see everything else unfolding in time through all duration. When an eternal being communicates what it can see of the section of time we call "future" to a being in time, we call the recipient a prophet or a diviner, and babble about predestination -- but it's not predestination; the eternal being is just sitting there at the finish line, seeing what we can't.

As for free will, one of the basic points of human existence is that of becoming free, starting out from a state of almost complete determination by heredity and environment and slowly expanding the "wiggle room" between these. You make your fate in future lives by the choices you make in this one; your destiny is what your higher self wants, and it's possible for you to screw up and not achieve it. So the freedom of the will is real, even though it's not absolute, and even though many human beings never get around to exercising it.

replies.title: Re: The nature of time
replies.content: Thanks.  I hadn't considered the possibility of two classes of beings.

replies.title: Re: The nature of time
replies.content: Would the mental and spiritual planes be eternal then? If so, it makes my head hurt trying to understand how we can develop mental bodies, as they'd already be there....

replies.title: Re: The nature of time
replies.content: That's the claim in some sources. I'm in the process of rethinking that as I study more old occult philosophy.

replies.title: Re: The nature of time
replies.content:
I also see this very much along the lines JMG describes. If I can introduce another piece of technical vocabulary, it might help.

Philosophers like to distinguish being "sempiternal" from being "eternal" in the most strict sense. Eternal, as JMG rightly states, means existing outside the flow of time, whereas sempiternal means existing within time at every moment.

As an analogy, think of time as an old-fashioned movie reel. Ordinary temporally-limited things (which are neither eternal nor sempiternal) are in some, but not all, of the frames of the movie. Sempiternal things are in every frame of the movie. Eternal things are not part of the movie at all; they are outside, able to see the entire movie reel laid out on the wall in front of them, all at once. So in this analogy, the guy who works in the projection booth or the editing room is equivalent to the "eternal": he is not within the movie at all, but rather, apart from and outside the whole thing.

Again, I hope this is helpful!

comments.title: Rock Shop Visit, Part 2
comments.content:
Hello,

A thank you to you, JMG, and to Petra and others for your responses on my rock shop visit a few weeks ago. https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/61730.html?thread=5051682#cmt5051682

A few follow up notes.

Just a few days after that, I coincidentally (ha!), saw in my local newspaper an ad for a rock show being put on by a local gem and mineral club that very weekend. Because of your reassurances that my experience was probably due to one bad apple in the industry, I decided to go and take my daughter. It was a lovely, family-friendly event. I was able to pick up a number of stones and gems mentioned in ENM and my daughter was gifted more pretty rocks than she could carry. No weird vibes – all nice people. Thank you!

Also, I got curious and decided to research the shop were the weird incident happened. Interestingly, they do almost no marketing at all. That’s unusual these days. But to add to that, on all the testimonial sites (Yelp, FB, etc) they have amazingly good ratings that seem to go beyond statistical possibilities. No average or short ratings, everyone writes a paragraph about how they LOVE the place, can’t wait to go back and oh yeah, you should go there too. Also, many mentioned that their children couldn't stop talking about it. One even said his kid was “mesmerized”.

If I can make an amateur hypothesis, the owner of this shop seems to have put some sort of enchantment over the place that overpowers people’s free will to make an opinion about his shop and then has them do his marketing and customer recruitment for him, with an extra focus on kids getting their parents to take them there and spend lots of money. My clumsy SOP practice was enough to set off alarm bells for me about this nefarious activity and get us out of there before the enchantment was put on us. Does that seem right?

And to the other commenter, yes, it seems it was optical calcite that the owner was using to do his little mesmerizing routine on the kids.

Thanks!

comments.title: SoP and Initiation
comments.content: Following your comment that somebody can do the SoP, meditation, and some form of divination and have a productive practice, where does the concept of initiation come in? In other words, in following the CGD and other paths, a person can be initiated -- and even self-initiate -- into that particular tradition. But what about if following the above suggestion? Is that a factor? Or is initiation even a thing to be sought after?

replies.title: Re: SoP and Initiation
replies.content: The word "initiation" literally means "beginning." Initiation in a magical sense is the process of beginning work in a particular form or mode of magic; you can do it by going through a ceremony and then doing the work, or you can just do the work. The ceremony, if it's done competently and resonates with you, can make the early phases of the work a little easier and a little more productive -- but that's all it does. You can get the same place yourself by patient hard work, and given the state of the magical scene these days, that's generally the safer option.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I don't know of anything comparable. That's why I wrote it.

2) Well, to begin with, notice that believers in all the various monotheisms have experiences of this kind. Here's a Christian mystic with a vision telling her that faith in Jesus is the only way to heaven; here's a Muslim mystic with a vision telling him that faith in the teachings of the Quran is the only way to heaven. Both of them can't be right. Thus we know that religious experience can't always be taken at face value. Polytheists also have experiences that, for similar reasons, can't be taken at face value. Similarly, we all know about optical illusions and other reasons why the senses can't always be taken at face value; that doesn't mean that all sensory experiences are wrong. It simply means that we need to consider the testimony of our senses in the light of reason and experience -- and the same thing is true of religious experiences.

3) I have a very mixed and uncertain opinion of such things. Most of what I've seen is LARPing pretending to be religion -- but then I once successfully (and quite accidentally) invoked Batman, and I have reason to believe that at least some of the deities of the Cthulhu mythos as I've imagined them in The Weird of Hali would respond if prayed to. comments.title: Oaths
comments.content:
What are the karmic consequences of breaking an oath, eg, cheating on your wife (but she doesn't know) or a priest losing his faith in the god he promised to serve but he goes through the motions anyway because he likes the lifestyle?

If you swear an oath to God and break it, is there a spiritual penalty?

replies.title: Re: Oaths
replies.content: Yes, there's always a penalty. If you cheat on your wife, for example, you become the kind of person who cheats on his wife -- i.e., a sleazebag -- and that contaminates other aspects of your life; other people notice, subliminally or otherwise, and start treating you like a sleazebag -- and people start cheating on you, or simply cheating you, because they decide you're a sleazebag and deserve no better. Then you land in your next life and the same thing happens again, but the consequences put cleats on before they show up and work you over. Karma is character -- and character is destiny.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
(Same person as above - separate to ease deletion if JMG decides so.)

Some points I think bear making at the entry level if possible:

1) calling the entire current country "Mexico" was imperialism - "Mexihco" had meant only current Central Mexico, and the "Mexihcas" were the people only 2 of the city-states in it, one of which engaged in a great, rapid expansion and made its language the lingua franca futher beyond ("Guatemala" comes from the Nahuatl translation of a Maya-language name - most Guatemalans are Maya-descended - the Nahua/Mexihca merchants formed a spy network, as did the Venetians, Mongols, and I'm sure others). For physical-geography/technology reasons, the Mexihcas were roughly at the limit of their plausible expansion when the Spaniards arrived, but then it was possible for the (elite, part-Spanish/part-Nahua) occupiers of Central Mexico to continue the centralization that expanded over the whole current country (formerly New Spain) in the colonial period, causing the new country to be named after its imperial capital.

2) the conflict outside people most motice today (besides any rebel-chic factor attached to the Zapatistas - I say this because people who talk about them generally don't pay attention to intra-Mexican divisions in general) is between the more Spanish/more plugged into the US economy Northern Mexicans and the center, but consider also: Mayas are not "almost identical to" Nahuas; Maya resistance to Mexihca, Spanish, *and modern Mexican* imperialism went into the 20th century; Nahua soldiers were the bulk of the forces that conquered Mexico beyond the center (many having been non-Mexihca fully voluntary Nahua allies to the Spaniards against the Mexihcas; Indian Conquistadors may be of interest); the Mexihcas were starving Tlaxcallan out, having a cold war with Tzintzuntzán and economically overpowering the Mayas they didn't beat militarily at the same time when the Spanish arrived; "race-traitor" Malintzin was a sex slave *before* the Spanish showed up (to Mayas); currently most of Central Mexico speaks Castillian and discriminates against Amerindian-language speakers, including the speakers of Nahuatl - from Central Mexico - that keep it in West Mexico (i.e. West Mexico once had Nahuatl imposed on it by the center - now the center, having upgraded to a richer empire, would like to impose Castillian); and, of course, there's plenty of Amerindian languages neither Nahua nor Maya still spoken by fairly large amounts of people.

3) Aztlan (north of Mexihco/Central Mexico, from which various groups including the Mexihca seem to have migrated) probably wasn't in the US, and I think not even close to the border - the migrations of (horseless) nomads to Central Mexico can have been over large distances for the time and place without having started near the US. In any case, the part of New Spain/Mexico that the US took was mostly held only on paper, and many occupiers were Amerindians affiliated to neither country - even in current southern California/New Mexico/Texas where there were people of Spanish descent, they were doing no good job of integrating the nearest Amerindians (see: Pueblo Revolt). Whoever wants to feel vengeful over the US-Mexico border has a right to, but should admit they feel it for a Castillian-speaking culture, and more physically descended from actual Spanish the closer to the border, instead of anything that has anything to do with the native cultures (the large ones - smaller peoples were near the border, and would much rather have nothing to do with *either* country).

So whoever tells you "Mexicans are one people" wants to sell you something. The something may even be good, but that statement isn't one of a current fact, and I think trying to make it so would involve unpleasant processes (some of the people in favor of them won't acknowledge). If you considered any of the above alt-Rightist-sounding, making you suspicious of my recommendations, keep in mind that AskHistorians is a Left-Liberal bastion, I'm pretty sure the author of the History of Latin America podcast is also Left-Liberal, and I don't know what Xavier Marquez is exactly, but I'm pretty sure not alt-Rightist. I don't bring this up to suggest any of them would endorse this comment, but against any risk that you'd disregard the previous because of this one.

(And I know this can be argued to be not quite on-topic, but I thought I should warn strongly against equating Mexihcas/Aztecs/Nahuas/Mesoamericans/Mexicans.)

FWIW, my current understanding of gods is that there's a level below the Archetypes and above the egregore level where you get, shall we say, less hallowed expressions of gods. (Zeus's philandering is an, er, Classic example, and there's at least one version of two-thirds of the Christian Trinity that comes off as the Abusive Father and Abused Son.) If this is correct, I'd call the Aztec deities an extreme version of this phenomenon.

Two caveats: First, of course, it's possible that there's no distinction between this and the demonic, and I've considered "from the point of view of the astral ecosystem at least some of the entities we call demons may just be predators to which we are prey" a plausible hypothesis for years now. Second, I wouldn't be surprised if there's effects from the underlying land in play here: given the structure of the Maya calendar I feel pretty comfortable saying that, much like (possibly exactly like) the Great Plains to the north, Mesoamerica pushes towards a fractal view of spacetime; given that, I'd expect "men are to the gods as maize is to men" to be buttressing Mesoamerican human sacrifice traditions. (The sense I got from the Great Plains tended closer to "as men raise pets, so gods raise men"... and now that I think about it I wonder if that's fueling some of the uplifted animal stories in American sci-fi...)

comments.title: Asperger's and Tarot
comments.content:
Dear John Michael Greer:

Thank you again for hosting Magic Monday. I have a couple questions this week.

1) Like you I also have Asperger's syndrome, and if you don't mind me asking, what challenges has this presented for your magical practice? What advice would you give to the aspiring student with Asperger's (e.g. concerning perfectionism, poor vizualization, and improving one's concentration?)
2) Sometime ago I bought the Smith-Waite Centennial Tarot Deck, I love the aged/grayed look of the cards, along with the biography and sample artwork of Pamela "Pixie" Coleman-Smith. A friend advised though that for visionary work in a ceremonial magic context that I should use a more brightly colored edition of the Smith-Waite deck. Would the centennial edition I have still be good for divination, or should I use the same deck for both practices?
Thank you in advance for your time and consideration, and advice or response is appreciated. Have a wonderful evening.

Sincerely,
Anon.

replies.title: Re: Asperger's and Tarot
replies.content:
1) Aspergers syndrome is actually a huge advantage for the aspiring occultist. Most Aspies are comfortable with solitude, and magic is largely a solitary practice, and the famous Aspie habit of fanatic concentration on obscure bodies of knowledge makes learning occult philosophy and symbolism a breeze. Most Aspies are very good at visual imagery -- I'm somewhat odd in that I think solely in spoken language. If you're not, regular practice will take care of it, and also build your capacity for concentration to levels that neurotypical people find frightening.

2) I'd encourage you to try visionary work with both, and see which works better for you -- one size emphatically does not fit all, and if garish colors are as hard on your nervous system as they are on mine, you may find the Centennial edition gives you better results. In any case, you should certainly use whichever version you like better for divinatory purposes, as that will help you build a rapport with the deck and give better readings as a result.

replies.title: Re: Asperger's and Tarot
replies.content:
John; What's odd about an Aspie thinking primarily in spoken language? Or language in print. It's quite common among us women. As is obsessive fanfic writing and the like, at least in the science fiction crowd. And many of us can talk the ears off a donkey. But visualization? Takes an enormous amount of work. Even now the (highly desert-and-mountain based)* imagery I wrote myself for my enriched LBPR) as accompanied by a verbal soundtrack to make the pictures come clear.

*Or why it was coming out so feeble here. Back to unadorned Golden Dawn basics until the land spirits speak to me.

replies.title: Re: Asperger's and Tarot
replies.content: The great majority of the Aspies I've met, female as well as male, think in visual terms rather than verbal ones -- thus the preeminence of Aspies in mathematics, much of which is easiest to grasp if you've got a visual imagination. Thus my comment; among the Aspies I've known, my mental patterns make me an outlier. Of course your mileage may vary.

replies.title: Re: Asperger's and Tarot
replies.content:
I find this comment fascinating, since I'm autistic and also think nearly entirely in spoken language, and or mathematical equations. I've got no visual imagination at all, and yet find mathematics extremely easy and intuitive.

Fascinating.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I have a somewhat odd question, but here goes. I'm autistic, and when I was diagnosed my parents were told to expect me to need to be placed in a home by the time I was a teenager. When I tell people this, people who know someone on the spectrum tend to go "Oh, that makes sense!", but lots of people flat out won't believe it because I seem to have a capacity to pick up on social cues.

What's interesting is that there are certain classes of cues I simply can't pick up on (I have zero idea if someone is flirting with me, for example), but in plenty of other cases I seem to be more attentive to social cues than most neurotypicals.

I also have quite a few psychic abilities (nothing flashy, but fairly distinctive): I've got dream precognition, I can sense where to go to meet someone, among others. This seems to suggest that I'm using psychic abilities to partially make up for a damaged nervous system.

1) Do you know of anyone else who has done something like this?

2) How can I go about strengthen my psychic abilities?

3) If I pursue a course of magical work, I've heard that both psychism and autism strengthen the effects: would the combination be even more potent?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First, congrats to your parents for not institutionalizing you. I know people who had that done to them and it really messed them up.

1) I know other people on the spectrum who can catch some social cues -- my wife is one of them. In her case it was obsessive training by a seriously neurotic mother, but her family also has an inherited strain of psychism.

2) There are many ways to do this and most will mess you over. I recommend starting with the basics of magical training -- a daily banishing ritual such as the Sphere of Protection (which I've covered in posts on this journal), discursive meditation, and a daily divination. Once you have that set of practices down, and are doing all three daily, W.E Butler's How to Read The Aura, Practice Psychometry, Telepathy, and Clairvoyance would be a useful guide -- Butler was a first-rate practical occultist and so knew how to teach using methods that are generally safe.

3) It really depends on how hard you work. That combination of factors will tend to give you a head start, but a student who starts without those advantages and puts the time into daily study and practice will soon surpass a student who has those advantages and just dawdles around.

comments.title: A Sworn Oath
comments.content:
Greetings, Mr. Greer.


Recently my son and I were watching the 2010 remake of Clash of Titans. Certainly not the best representation of the Greek myths, but it served its purpose of entertaining, as well as introducing, my 9 year old son to polytheism.

At one point in the movie, the humans pull down a colossus of Zeus in a futile attempt to rebel against the Gods. Watching that filled my son with indignation. Much like myself, he values ancient artifacts and cultural relics. He then asked how someone could do something like that. I stood up with energy, and declared "Someone would have to send me to the Gods, rather than topple one of their statues!". Right then I felt a strong surge of energy enter my body, and I felt a strong sense of pride and allegiance. My son looked on in awe for a moment, and then agreed that he couldn't do such a thing either.

My question is, was the surge of energy I felt the direct result of swearing an oath? I felt stamped afterward, particularly by Zeus or the Olympian gods. That by saying those words, I swore allegiance to the Olympians and they reciprocated. If what I did was an oath, is there a real possibility that the scenario I swore by will come to pass? If so, then I plan to uphold it but I definitely wanted to get your opinion on this.

replies.title: Re: A Sworn Oath
replies.content: Not an oath, exactly, but certainly a pledge of loyalty and love. I don't think it's likely that any such scenario will come to pass, no, but the gods know what's in your heart and will reward you accordingly.

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

1) In my occult readings, I've come across the idea that bad karma can possibly be encountered and defused in the psychic realms of Yesod, before it manifests in Malkuth. If so, how does that work? Nothing you've written about karma suggests that there's a shortcut out of it - one way or another you have to address the consequences of your actions.

2) Within the universe of the Weird of Hali novels, is there an afterlife? It doesn't seem to get addressed, making me think that the characters view death as a permanent thing.

-Cliff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) One way or another, you're going to experience the consequences of your actions, but you can do it on any plane -- that's one of the great practical secrets that WW Atkinson hints at in the Kybalion, among other places. Yesod is more challenging than most, as you have to have adequate control of the life force, but you can do it in the emotional sphere -- by coming to feel with absolute brutal clarity the consequences of your actions -- or in the mental sphere -- by understanding them with the same gut-punch clarity. It takes work to do this, though.

2) Novels and afterlives rarely work well together, as you have to have the threat and sorrow of death to give the events of the story poignancy. Still, the answer's hinted at in one place -- the complicated origins of Justin's son Robin...

comments.title: Some banishing ritual questions
comments.content:
Hey hey JMG,

First, thank you very much for putting up the Sphere of Protection posts. I asked for them and I greatly appreciate it. I suspect others do as well.

1) Some of the elements don't have as much of a sensation as the others seem to and I'm wondering what I can do to strengthen them. Example: When I do the green gate of the tall stones I always feel the Earth beneath me and and the solid nature of it in my legs, sort of flowing into me. It is a very pleasant sensation. But I really don't get anything from the red gate. Is there anything that I can do to work on this?

2) When I first I didn't use any gods. When I decided to add gods I thought through who to use and I came up with Odin the allfather, Jord the earth mother, Bauldr the shining one. I've changed it to Odin, Frigga, and either Bauldr or Heimdall. Bauldr seems the more logical choice, but I feel more drawn to Heimdall. Also, the trinity of their characteristics is appealing. Odin is very wise, Frigga is wise and has foreknowledge, and Heimdall is wise and far sighted. The three of them seem like a good place to go when seeking wisdom, but is the SoP the place to do it?

3) Hypothetical question, what would happen if a significant part of the country started do a daily banishing ritual? 10% of the population, half? Would that be enough to fix this country's tainted egregore?

4) You said in a previous post the the LBRP was a little harsher than the SoP. Can you explain a little about that? If I switch to the LBRP, which I am probably going to do because it is part of Learning Ritual Magic and after some run ins with the watcher I think I am ready to go on, is there anything that I need to know about it? I assume that harsher doesn't translate to dangerous in any way, but I would rather ask then be surprised by something.

Thanks,
Tim

replies.title: Re: Some banishing ritual questions
replies.content:
1) Keep working at it. Most people find some elements easier than others.

2) Pray to them, and offer them each some ale, then go for it.

3) Nobody knows. Seriously, nobody knows.

4) It's not a matter of danger. The SoP has a gentle feeling, the LBRP is as sharp and jagged as a pentagram.

replies.content:
I know it's after midnight. I just wanted to say thanks again and to let you know that I did head your advice:

2) follow up. I did pray them and asked if it was ok to invoke them and offered them some ale before I got started. I actually looked for mead but couldn't find any in town. I'm brand spanking new at talking to the gods, but the answer seemed to be approval as best as I can tell.

Thanks,
Tim

People keep asking me how I'll do things, like get directions, check the news, find restaurants, etc. What's really weird is that I explain it, but it's like I never said anything.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No response is required, but here it is.

My experience is with people in their mid-20s: I don't dare talk about internet use with anyone over about 30 or so since there's the risk of violent (one time literally) reactions, but I'm fairly open with my peers, many of whom hate the internet, and desperately want off.

Some of the "but what about x" is legitimate and I have no answer for it (job applications are nearly all being done online where we live, for example). But much of it I do have an answer for: news, newspapers; keeping in touch with people, phones and letters; finding restaurants, phone-books, etc.

It's as if the idea of doing something offline is unthinkable to many of my peers though, and most of them have no illusions that Progress is either inevitable nor good. So I doubt it's the hold of progress, but there's something really weird here....

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I've seen some discussion of marriage oaths here, and I have a weird question: I have two close friends who got married when they were eighteen. Part of this was they were childhood best friends, and they both sincerely love each other, as friends; and part of it was family pressure, but as lovers, they don't work. They both want to try to make it work, and have turned to me for advice on how to do that*. Both of them have confessed that they think the marriage was a mistake, but they don't want to hurt each other and feel bound by their oaths.

I'm planning to sit both of them down and tell them that it isn't working for either of them, and suggest that they break up and pursue a relationship as good friends instead. Karmically, what would be the risks for them if they pursued such a path? I suspect that they will need to deal with the fact that they messed their life up by getting into a poorly thought out marriage in the first place, but in such a situation, are there risks with breaking their marriage oaths?

*Before anyone says anything about it, they're asking me because we grew up together, and all three of us have always been very, very close. They both trust me, and they both know that the other is turning to me for advice, although I won't say what I said without express permission.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A marriage oath is a promise between two people. If both parties want to release the promise, and do so freely, the karma that results is positive -- a mistake was made, it was understood, it was corrected, both people accept it and release each other from a mistaken bond: that produces a karmic pattern of wisdom and kindness.

It would be a good idea to do at least a little ceremony to unbind the vows, in which each of them says to the other, "I release you from your vows and I thank you for every good thing you've done for me," or something of that nature, and the ring or rings get given back to the giver(s). That way the magical dimension of the vows is taken care of as well.

comments.content:
After a hiatus from magical practice brought about by overly arrogant attempts at more expansive magical practices I am getting back into practice by working through the recent instructions you posted on the SOP. Today I did the second session of the element of air, so I got a couple months ahead of me to get the whole ritual developed. As I go I am making slight customization to my own spirituality. There is trial and error, but I wanted to share the current form of it and ask your thoughts on it.

During the opening I am invoking three entities which have each appeared to me unbidden once, and which I have conversed with in meditation slightly. First an almost middle aged woman in blue robes who appeared to be with an air of wisdom and compassion, she hasn't really shared her name, so at present I call her Blue Woman. Second a woman who appeared to me when I was walking in the desert and prayed for support during a fit of melancholy who has the color and aroma of Rabbit Brush, who I address as Rabbit Brush or Chimisa. Third another woman, younger and dressed in red who gave me a kiss (friendly, moving, but not sexual) in a dream I call Charm. The visualizations and motions are all standard, and it feels pretty good. Still I don't really know who they are except very vaguely, so that's kinda weird. I pray "guide me, protect me, and bless me, today and always." the order is based on how I feel the three entities respectively have shown inclination to offer me favors in the past experiences.

During the opening of the air gate I said something like "By the yellow gate of the rushing winds and the far seeing hawk I invoke the Air, its gods, its spirits, and its powers. May the powers of Air bless and protect me this day and always, and further my work. May my mind be inspired by the ways of nature." for the invocation closing with the given "I thank the powers of air for their gifts.", then the banishing is also standard at present. The visualizations are as you wrote them near as possible, though I note that the wind during the banishing blows awat from me to the east, like the funk is being pulled through the gate and then lifted in a great wind beyond the horizon.

Finally to close the ritual I am using the Serenity Prayer, which is a personal favorite. Grant me the serenity to accept the things I cannot change, the courage to change the things I can, and the wisdom to know the difference.


Does this seem like a promising tact to take for the SOP, and is it appropriate to continue making slight adjustments as I go through the slow learning initiation based on what feels more or less resonate?

I plan on starting the Dolmen Arch once it's published (I feel drawn to AODA spirituality, and got a good divination for pursuing it), and have a few questions: I assume I can get a bit of a head-start on the Dolmen Arch by joining the AODA and getting to work there. Is this correct?

If the Dolmen Arch uses the SoP, which version would it use? Ideally I'd like to avoid switching if I can just stick with one form of it. Does it assign names to use, or is it the free for all that normally comes up?

Does it assign a divination oracle? If not, can I continue to use my tarot cards?

Finally, are there other steps I can use to get a head start on the process, so that when the book is published I've done as much of the prep work as possible?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm going to challenge your entire approach here. Don't try to get a head start. Instead, I'd encourage you to approach the book as a beginner, with what zen masters call "beginner's mind" -- that openness to new teachings that makes for deep learning. If you want to join AODA, by all means, but do it for its own sake, not in order to get ahead of yourself with the Dolmen Arch. The DA course is designed for the complete beginner; be that complete beginner, and you'll get the most from it.

comments.title: About my second pathwork ever
comments.content:
Before this MM is over, I had an interesting second pathworking session. On the first one I found some interesting imagery which checked out with the path (Tau). I also found a hooded being inside a small atrium with four pillars that had the magical tools. It was holding a square slab of stone and carving something on it. When questioning for validity, he looked at me from within the shadow of his hood, with sparkling white eyes. As a response he showed me he was carving the letter Tau on the slab. Satisfied I thanked him and went back.

After two meditations on it and a failed attempt due to indigestion, I tried another session today. I didn't know if I should visit the same place, but I tried it. I tried to summon a guide and I saw a huge fly-like spirit. Now I am not sure if I tested it, but perhaps I might have gone with my gut and ignored it. Then I got a tap on my shoulder and a Golem or suit of armor with an indigo sash presented itself. I tested it, and it checked out. But when it guided me it just kind of repeated what I had known from my meditations. Then it took me on a new path that led to some kind of infinite flatland. I felt skeptical so I tested the spirit again and it hesitated. So I banished him with the Pentagram and headed back.

In retrospect I might have dismissed the true guide (the insectoid) and went with a phony. An interesting experience.
